claim: ISO 19867-1:2018 and IWA clean-cooking standards define performance tiers for thermal efficiency, emissions, and safety, but compliance verification through independent testing is absent for most stoves sold and deployed in LMICs.

source.title: ISO 19867-1:2018 Clean cookstoves and clean cooking solutions

method: Reviewed ISO standards documentation, performance-tier definitions, compliance-verification requirements, and implementation status across LMICs.
limitations: ISO standards define testing protocols but do not mandate independent verification — manufacturers can self-certify without third-party testing.
Compliance-verification systems vary enormously by country — some have national testing laboratories while others have no verification infrastructure.
The standards define laboratory test protocols that may not reflect field conditions — fuel type, moisture, cooking practices, and maintenance all differ between lab and household.
Compliance data is not systematically aggregated across countries — no global database of stove compliance status exists.

claim: Household air pollution from solid-fuel cooking causes an estimated 3.2 million premature deaths annually, with the burden concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia where clean-cooking adoption rates remain below 20 percent.
method: Reviewed WHO fact sheet for mortality estimates, exposure data, and clean-cooking adoption statistics.
limitations: The 3.2 million figure is a modeled estimate from exposure-response functions, not direct mortality counts — it depends on exposure measurement methodology.
Clean-cooking adoption rates are self-reported in household surveys — 'adoption' may mean ownership rather than exclusive use.
The burden estimate does not distinguish between partial and complete fuel transition — households using both clean and traditional stoves have intermediate exposure.
Sub-national variation in adoption rates is documented for some countries but not systematically aggregated.

claim: Systematic field-performance monitoring covers fewer than 5 percent of deployed stoves in most LMIC countries, meaning that the actual health impact of stove programs is estimated from laboratory data and exposure models rather than verified field measurements.

source.title: Clean cooking field monitoring: global coverage assessment

method: Reviewed Global Alliance for Clean Cookstoves monitoring documentation, field-assessment methodology, and coverage-rate estimates across countries.
limitations: The 'fewer than 5 percent' figure is an aggregate — some programs have better monitoring while others have none.
Field-monitoring methodology varies — some use kitchen-performance tests while others use temperature sensors or emission monitors.
Monitoring coverage changes over time — some countries have expanded field assessment since the assessment.
The assessment does not capture informal or private-sector stove deployments that may have no monitoring at all.
